title: First-Class and Higher-Order Functions

learningObjectives.text: Explain functions as values using a new example and justify the result.
Explain callbacks using a new example and justify the result.
Explain functions returning functions using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: The ideas in first-class and higher-order functions recur throughout later JavaScript work, reducing memorization and debugging later. It belongs to the **Data Structures and Functional Techniques** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Functions as values** → **Callbacks** → **Functions returning functions**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Functions as values
sections.body: Functions are first-class values: they can be stored, passed, and returned. Referring to a function is different from invoking it.

sections.title: Callbacks
sections.body: A callback is a function supplied so another function can invoke it at the appropriate time or for each value. Understand whether it runs now or later.

sections.title: Functions returning functions
sections.body: A function can return a configured function that remembers creation-time context. This supports factories and reusable behavior.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: A function returns a configured callback that is then passed to `filter`.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: First-Class and Higher-Order Functions connects Functions as values, Callbacks, Functions returning functions.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: A learner must use First-Class and Higher-Order Functions in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Apply Functions as values deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of First-Class and Higher-Order Functions.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of First-Class and Higher-Order Functions.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of First-Class and Higher-Order Functions.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving First-Class and Higher-Order Functions?

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of First-Class and Higher-Order Functions.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of First-Class and Higher-Order Functions.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Functions as values, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of First-Class and Higher-Order Functions.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use First-Class and Higher-Order Functions?

choices.label: Avoid First-Class and Higher-Order Functions completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of First-Class and Higher-Order Functions.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of First-Class and Higher-Order Functions.

choices.label: Use First-Class and Higher-Order Functions when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Use First-Class and Higher-Order Functions in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of First-Class and Higher-Order Functions.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
